title: Problem 1.1 (Erdős)
statement_text: Let $k\geq 4$. For every $n\in\mathbb{N}$ let $f_{k}(n)$ denote the largest integer $r\geq 1$ such that there exists a $(k,r)$-graph on $n$ vertices ($f_{k}(n):=0$ if no such graph exists for any $r\geq 1$). Is it true that $f_{k}(n)\rightarrow\infty$ for $n\rightarrow\infty$?
context_text: Erdős posed this in 1985 as a strong version of Dirac's conjecture, asking whether for every $k\geq 4$ and $r\geq 1$ there exists a $k$-vertex-critical graph in which no set of at most $r$ edges is critical. The case $r=1$ corresponds to Dirac's conjecture. The problem has been reiterated in several books and problem collections (cf. page 66 of [3] and [4, 7]).
paper_contribution: Solves the problem affirmatively for all $k\geq 5$, proving $f_k(n)=\Omega(n^{1/3})$; the case $k=4$ remains open.
paper_title: Critical edge sets in vertex-critical graphs

title: Problem 5.2
context_text: This question is posed in relation to the still-open case $k=4$ of Erdős' problem. A $(4,1)$-graph is a $4$-vertex-critical graph containing no critical edge; the question asks whether a $6$-regular example of such a graph exists, motivated by structural properties of $4$-vertex-critical graphs established in the paper.
paper_title: Critical edge sets in vertex-critical graphs
